title: Rusk Making
description: In a chai-guzzling nation like India, there can never be enough tea-time snack options. Rusk, is one of them. Derived from the word 'rosca', which is Portuguese for 'a twisted, coiled roll of bread', a rusk is a rectangular, hard, dry biscuit prepared by double-baking bread. Rusk are popular bakery products among elderly and working class which supplements for food for many. These Rusk have longer shelf life can be kept for longer period of time. Manufacturing process of Rusk is similar to bread manufacturing except for baking of sliced bread of different sizes as desired. Rusk are mostly prepared from local bakeries except for few branded Rusk majority are available from local bakeries. One and a big reason for their popularity in India would be the affordable and economical nature of these biscuits. Apart from having existed in our country for closer to centuries now, it is their budget pricing that has remained constant till now that makes it such a popular snack.

title: French Fries
description: French fries are among the highest saleable potato products. This is the most abundant processed potato and can be found in many varieties such as lattice cut, wedges, curly, batter dipped, seasoned, or straight-cut including French Fries on menu is one of the easiest ways to increase sales and profits for the companies. Indian fast food sector is growing at 25-30% annually due to rapid growth of fast food chain both Indian and international. Presently, the core food service Indian market is 3600 crores and the share of quick service restaurants is Rs. 2500 crores. Out of the total snacks, potato based products like French fries, wedges, products using potato flakes and other Indian snacks have about 30% share in fast food industry, approximately 75 crores in value terms.

title: Bread Making
description: The bakery industry is very popular in urban part of India. Around 60% of production takes place in the unorganized sector. There are around 2 mn unorganized bakeries in the country, comprising small bakery units, cottage and household type manufacturing. Bakery products are part of the processed food category. They include cake, pastries, biscuits, bread, breakfast cereals, and other products. Bakery products generally consumed as supplement foods or breakfast food in between the meal times. Bread and other bakery products are now consumed in almost in every Indian home and hotel. Bread also used to prepare sandwiches, bread toast, bread rolls, breadcrumbs etc. A huge consumption of bakery products because of mainly two reasons, firstly, they are ready-to-eat or convenience food to eat anywhere at any time and secondly, they can satisfy appetite.

title: Biscuit Making
description: Bread and Biscuits form the major baked foods accounting to over 80% of total bakery products produced in the country. The quantities of bread and biscuits produced are more or less same, however, value of biscuits is 112 times more than bread. The industry has traditionally been and largely continues to be in the unorganized sector contributing to over 70% of the total production. Biscuit making is a conventional activity in many parts of the country. Despite the advent of modern, large capacity and automatic biscuit making plants, large section of people especially in semi-urban and rural areas still prefer fresh biscuits from local bakery as they are cheap and offer many varieties. These manufacturers are able to cater to some typical local palate as well. Thus, they are able to withstand competition from organised sector units.

title: Jam & Jelly
description: Jam, Jelly and Pickles are important food products as they enhance palatability of food due to its taste and flavour. They are prepared from fruits and they supplement the food with vitamins and minerals. There are many types of pickles available in India. These products have very good market potential as they are consumed in most of the households, restaurants, other eateries, clubs, hostels etc. They are sold through many outlets like grocery shops, departmental stores etc. But these products are very popular in the North-East region of India and the consumption is increasing year after year. Presently, there are limited numbers of small units producing these products in the region and there is a wide gap between demand and supply which is met by the manufacturers from outside the region.

title: Macroni
description: Macaroni or gold finger is a simple product made from Maida and Tapioca flour. This is a mass consumption item especially amongst children and youngsters. The product comes under Ready-to-Eat category and has become very popular throughout the country. Macaroni means a product made from a blend of flours. It is manufactured in different sizes, hollow or solid and for different cooking methods. Some are made for cooking whereas others for frying. It is one of the cheap eatables available in the market as compared to potato wafers, cheese balls or other such items in the same category. Its popularity in urban markets is limited but semi-urban and rural markets have witnessed substantial increase in demand. Since they enjoy considerable shelf-life, they are stored in households in large quantities. It has become a very popular snack food or lunch-box item. Since it is not a very costly item, people from all walks of life can afford it.

title: Soya Paneer
description: Soya Milk is an inexpensive and remarkably versatile high protein food made from soyabeans. It is a white liquid made from the seed. Unlike most other protein foods, milk is entirely free from cholesterol and low in fat specially saturated fats. The quality of protein is as high as that found in chicken. It is also good for dieters as this contain low calories. It is an excellent food for babies, children, elderly people and pregnant and lactating women since it contains vegetable protein which is very nutritious and easy to digest. Soya milk and its derivatives are the cheapest source of protein, its derivatives tofu (soya paneer) makes tasty dishes like matar paneer, palak paneer etc. and snacks like soya burger, patties, sandwiches, pakoras etc. and also used in desserts. Soya paneer is made from soya milk which is a rich source of high quality proteins and vitamins. Paneer is made by coagulating soya bean milk. Soya bean is a leguminous crop and is rich in proteins. Many value-added products are made from it like milk, sauce, paneer etc. Soya products are increasingly becoming popular especially amongst health conscious people.

title: Ice Cream Manufacturing
description: Ice Cream is consumed as Dessert as well as anytime snacks. It was considered as a luxury food for summer season only. However, with the development of Dairy and Milk processing industry it has also found use as a Dessert. The Bureau of Indian Standards have formulated IS2802:1964 for performed Ice Cream and IS5839:1970 for hygienic conditions for manufacture storage and sale of Perfumed Ice Cream. Ice-cream is typically regarded as a seasonal product, with demand peaking during the summer months. Winter witnesses a sharp decline in consumption and resultant dip in sales and profits. However, with the marketplace teeming with a host of innovative frozen variants, which consumers are enjoying even in the cold months, there is a surge in winter sales. Ice Cream is consumed as Dessert as well as anytime snacks.

title: Ready To Eat Noodle
description: Ready-to-eat noodles or instant noodles are one of the most popular convenience foods in India. The Indian instant noodles market has grown significantly driven by changing lifestyles, urbanization, and the convenience factor. Major brands like Maggi, Yippee, and Top Ramen dominate the market but there is substantial scope for small and medium enterprises. Manufacturing involves mixing wheat flour (maida) with water and other ingredients like salt and starch, sheeting the dough, cutting into noodle strands, steaming at high temperature, and either frying or air-drying for preservation. The noodles are then packed with seasoning or flavor sachets. The Indian instant noodles market is valued at over Rs. 12000 crores and growing at 10-12% annually. Small-scale manufacturing units can start with relatively low investment of Rs. 10-15 lakhs and cater to local and regional markets with unique flavors and competitive pricing. Raw materials include refined wheat flour, edible oil, salt, spices, starch, and packaging material.

title: Packaged Drinking Water
description: With increasing health awareness and growing concerns about water contamination and waterborne diseases, packaged drinking water has become a daily necessity across India. The Indian packaged water market is one of the fastest growing segments in the food and beverage industry, growing at approximately 20% annually. The manufacturing process involves sourcing raw water from borewells or municipal supply, followed by multi-stage purification including sand filtration, activated carbon filtration, micron cartridge filtration, reverse osmosis (RO) treatment, UV disinfection, and ozonization for final sterilization. The purified water is then filled into food-grade PET bottles or pouches using automated filling machines. BIS certification with ISI mark as per IS 14543 is mandatory for selling packaged drinking water in India. Starting a packaged drinking water plant typically requires investment in RO plant, bottle blowing machine, filling and capping machine, labeling machine, and shrink wrapping equipment. The business offers excellent profit margins and year-round steady demand making it an attractive MSME venture.

title: Multi Fruit Juice & Squash
description: India is the second largest producer of fruits in the world after China, producing over 90 million tonnes annually. Despite this massive production, only about 2% of fruits are processed into value-added products like juices, squashes, concentrates, and pulps. This presents an enormous opportunity for fruit juice and squash manufacturing units. Multi fruit juice is prepared by extracting juice from various fruits like mango, guava, orange, pineapple, apple, grape, and pomegranate, then blending them in specific proportions with sugar, citric acid, and permitted preservatives. Squash is a concentrated fruit syrup that is diluted with water before consumption. The Indian fruit juice market is growing at 15-20% annually driven by health consciousness, rising disposable incomes, and preference for natural beverages over carbonated drinks. Manufacturing requires fruit pulper, pasteurizer, homogenizer, filling machine, and cold storage facility. Raw materials include fresh seasonal fruits, sugar, citric acid, pectin, and food-grade preservatives. The business can be started with investment of Rs. 15-25 lakhs for a small-scale unit.

title: Namkeen Making
description: Namkeen or savory snacks are an integral part of Indian food culture, consumed during tea time, festivals, celebrations, and as everyday munchies. The Indian namkeen market is estimated at over Rs. 30000 crores and growing rapidly at 15-18% per year. Popular varieties include bhujia, mixture, sev, chivda (poha mixture), moong dal, aloo bhujia, and various regional specialties. Manufacturing process involves preparing dough or batter from gram flour (besan), rice flour, or other flours mixed with spices and seasonings, then shaping through extruders or molds, deep frying in edible oil at controlled temperatures, draining excess oil, adding final seasoning, and packaging in nitrogen-flushed pouches for extended shelf life. Both organized brands like Haldiram, Bikaji, and Balaji as well as thousands of local manufacturers thrive in this market. A small-scale namkeen unit can be started with Rs. 5-10 lakhs investment covering equipment like dough mixer, extruder, fryer, seasoning drum, and packaging machine. The business has high profit margins of 25-40% and strong repeat customer demand.

title: Tomato Ketchup
description: Tomato ketchup is one of the most widely consumed condiments in India and globally. It is used as a dipping sauce, cooking ingredient, and accompaniment with snacks like samosa, pakora, french fries, burgers, sandwiches, and various fast foods. The Indian ketchup and sauce market is valued at over Rs. 4500 crores and growing at 18-20% annually, driven by the expanding fast food industry, changing food habits, and increasing preference for processed condiments. Manufacturing involves sorting and washing ripe tomatoes, pulping to extract juice, cooking the pulp with sugar, salt, vinegar, spices (onion, garlic, clove, cinnamon, black pepper), and permitted food colors and preservatives. The mixture is cooked at controlled temperatures until desired consistency is achieved, then hot-filled into glass bottles or flexible pouches. Key equipment includes tomato pulper, steam jacketed kettle, homogenizer, filling machine, and labeling machine. A small-scale tomato ketchup manufacturing unit can be established with investment of Rs. 8-15 lakhs and can produce 500-1000 kg per day.

title: Pickle Making
description: Pickle or achar is an essential part of Indian cuisine found in virtually every household across the country. India has a rich tradition of pickle making with hundreds of regional varieties using different fruits, vegetables, and spices. Popular pickle varieties include mango (aam ka achar), lemon (nimbu ka achar), mixed vegetable, green chili, garlic, ginger, amla (gooseberry), and various non-vegetarian pickles. The Indian pickle market is estimated at Rs. 4000-5000 crores with organized sector growing at 15% annually. Manufacturing process involves selecting and cleaning raw materials (raw mangoes, lemons, chilies, etc.), cutting into desired shapes, sun-drying or salt-curing to remove moisture, mixing with spices (mustard seeds, fenugreek, turmeric, red chili powder, asafoetida), adding mustard oil or sesame oil, and allowing the mixture to mature in ceramic or glass jars. Commercial production requires cutting machines, mixer grinders, oil heating kettles, filling machines, and proper packaging in glass jars or food-grade pouches. A small pickle making unit requires minimal investment of Rs. 2-5 lakhs making it one of the most accessible food processing businesses.

title: Soft Drinks
description: Soft drinks or carbonated beverages are non-alcoholic drinks that typically contain carbonated water, sweetener (sugar or artificial), natural or artificial flavoring, and food colors. The Indian soft drinks market is one of the largest in the world, valued at over Rs. 50000 crores and growing at 8-10% annually. While major multinational brands dominate the cola segment, there is significant opportunity for small manufacturers in flavored carbonated drinks, fruit-flavored sodas, and regional specialty beverages like jeera masala soda, lemon soda, and mango-flavored drinks. Manufacturing process involves preparing sugar syrup, adding flavoring concentrates and food-grade colors, mixing with treated water, carbonating the mixture by injecting carbon dioxide gas under pressure, and bottling in PET bottles or glass bottles using automated filling lines. Key equipment includes water treatment plant, syrup preparation tank, carbonator, bottle washer, filling machine, capping machine, and labeling machine. Quality control is essential to maintain consistent taste, carbonation level, and food safety standards. FSSAI license and BIS standards compliance are mandatory. A small-scale soft drink manufacturing unit can be started with investment of Rs. 10-20 lakhs.
